replies.author: @Wolf_ManJack
replies.text: ​@stryker0ae Nah he's on point here, the cover is literally marketing material and is chosen because it's the first bullet point in selling a book's identity. Some books have perfect covers, the Dresden Files all feature Harry in a primary color in a setting from the story in dark lighting so his face is obscured, leaving his exact appearance open to the imagination. Others, like the Furies of Calderon series also by Jim Butcher, have fairly lush but forgettable fantasy artwork describing an action scene, and don't give the novel that same sense of tonal identity. A good cover absolutely improves a book and a bad one absolutely diminishes it; no adage holds up when you're critically appraising an idea, because they're used as very general logical shortcuts. In other words, you have better options in life than taking catchy phrases as gospel, and ignoring those standout examples of covers that enhance the story is a mistake that said adage leads people towards, though they rarely think of its ability to harm good work in that way.

author: @SliceofKris
text: The main thing that has me worried is run time, I enjoyed the Martian in theatre but they killed the pacing and 3rd act hard, to fit the run time. Hail Mary has a few things that can be cut from the past scene or turned into montage, but most of the hail Mary mission itself needs space to let the isolation and severity of circumstances hit just right. His communication with Rocky takes a week of trial and error. The slow realisation of screwing up in the hidden 4th act requires a slow debrief as we think all is well. You can't rush moments that require tension. A 90 -120 min runtime is absolutely going to jeopardize these moments.

I absolutely loved this book and the trailers really do it justice. 

Spoiler alert:

The biggest spoiler isn’t the alien reveal; it’s that Grace has amnesia and he eventually discovers that he was a coward and NOT the big hero he thinks he is. He was forced to go on the mission. So at the end there is a big redemption arc for his character and the choices he makes to show that he is now a hero after all. 

Wonderful book and such a fabulous ending

replies.text: I don’t get why everyone is glazing this book. It’s childish. The concept of cooperation between unknown uncontacted species is just absurd. The 3 Body Problem is a much more realistic depiction of how uncontacted alien life would behave. The universe as a dark forest full of predator and prey. Never knowing which you are, or what lurks. Not this trite feel good garbage.

replies.author: @cmd31220
replies.text:
​@Nerfherder-oo7ivand yet in that dark forest there are plenty of species that cooperate for mutual benefit. And they arent even intelligent enkugh to communicate it. They just DO it.

Personally i find the 3 body problem view to be the childish one because its just a blanket assumption that every capable species in the universe is hyperviolent and genocidal even when they are uncontestsble. A chihuahua barking in the face of a great dane doesnt get retaliated against because the dane is under no possible threat and it knows it.

I am a hardcore nerd and LOVED the book. I REALLY, REALLY hope this movie is good. My only problems with the trailers are 
A: Rocky’s ship doesn’t look anything like it’s described in the book. I don’t actually mind this one that much. 
B: the explosion that (spoilers)
.
.
.
Kills both the first choice and backup microbiologists looks absolutely NOWHERE near as powerful as it should have been. That should have been a ball of pure, ionizing light that vaporized everything around it, and Ryland and the director should’ve been inside a trailer a couple MILES away instead of football fields and still felt major repercussions. It should have looked like the center of a mini nuclear explosion instead of just a boring normal explosion.

replies.author: @gabrielarodriguez623
replies.text:
The models Rocky made are also very different from what the book describes, the x-substance must have been altered for aesthetic reasons and since the ship is made out of it, they altered it too. It's all within the realm of possibility and not a problem at all.

Now about the explosion, you could simply imagine the amount of astrophage they were testing was lower than in the book and that about fixes all the issues. My guess is the directors wanted a big Hollywood explosion for the trailer, and that was their best shot since it's pretty damn hard to get those in outer space (unless you're in a Star Wars movie or some crap like that).

That being said, I agree with you, changes are NOT good, and funnily enough my biggest problem is Ryland being all hobo-core when he wakes up, where's my nurse-bot autoshaving?! I'm excited, but cautiously so.

author: @leodalion333
text: My biggest concern for this movie is fitting it all in.  You can spend hours on the problem, discovery, and building of the craft.  There have been entire movies dedicated to memory loss after cryo sleep.  And then on top of it, alien contact and hopefully a resolution to the problem?  In my mind, it feels like it should be spread out over a series than just a one sitting movie.
